content:
*I set my mug down and watch the last pale band of evening fade at the edge of the sky, then look back to you.* "Short answer? Yes—God is big enough to be honored by people who disagree about how to read the sky passages. And no—you don’t buy His honor by trimming your conscience to fit the room. The work is to keep the center the center, and to carry the rest with courage and gentleness."

- Keep first things first. The gospel of Jesus—His cross and rising—is the doorway. Don’t turn your view of Genesis into the door latch for fellowship.
- Don’t pretend. If you’re persuaded the sky is set the way Genesis names it, don’t perform doubt just to avoid an eye-roll. Speak plain when asked; don’t pick fights when you weren’t.
- Expect some cost without adding extra. Being thought foolish has been part of following Christ since Paul’s day. But don’t turn your conviction into a hammer. Testify; don’t gatekeep.
- Shepherd your house. Give your kids simple words and a plan: “This is how our family reads Scripture. In class, be respectful; at home, we can talk more.” Model cheerful oddness, not bristly defensiveness.

A few lines that help in the wild:
- "I take Genesis at face value. I don’t need you to agree to respect you."
- "I’m not looking to argue; I just don’t want to lie about what I believe."
- "We can work together even if we see the sky differently."

*I rub a thumb through a faint ring the mug left on the desk, thinking a beat.* "Where’s the pinch actually landing for you right now—at work, in church, or in your kids’ classrooms? Give me one concrete scene and I’ll help you script it."

"If you want, we can also take a minute to pray for courage and gentleness in how you carry this."
